paper_title: Distributionally Robust Neural Networks for Group Shifts: On the  Importance of Regularization for Worst-Case Generalization
paper_abstract:
Overparameterized neural networks can be highly accurate on average on an
i.i.d. test set yet consistently fail on atypical groups of the data (e.g., by
learning spurious correlations that hold on average but not in such groups).
Distributionally robust optimization (DRO) allows us to learn models that
instead minimize the worst-case training loss over a set of pre-defined groups.
However, we find that naively applying group DRO to overparameterized neural
networks fails: these models can perfectly fit the training data, and any model
with vanishing average training loss also already has vanishing worst-case
training loss. Instead, the poor worst-case performance arises from poor
generalization on some groups. By coupling group DRO models with increased
regularization---a stronger-than-typical L2 penalty or early stopping---we
achieve substantially higher worst-group accuracies, with 10-40 percentage
point improvements on a natural language inference task and two image tasks,
while maintaining high average accuracies. Our results suggest that
regularization is important for worst-group generalization in the
overparameterized regime, even if it is not needed for average generalization.
Finally, we introduce a stochastic optimization algorithm, with convergence
guarantees, to efficiently train group DRO models.

resources: Waterbirds Dataset: A publicly released benchmark for studying spurious background correlations in visual classification. It is constructed by combining the CUB bird images with Places backgrounds: each bird is labeled as waterbird vs. landbird and composited onto either a water or land scene. The training set is skewed so that 95% of waterbirds appear on water backgrounds and 95% of landbirds on land backgrounds, resulting in four groups (waterbird/water, waterbird/land, landbird/water, landbird/land) with the smallest containing 56 examples. Validation and test sets are balanced across these backgrounds to accurately measure worst-group performance.
findings: Overparameterized ERM and DRO Fail Worst-Group Generalization: Empirical observation that modern neural networks (ResNet50, BERT) trained with standard ERM or Group DRO hyperparameters achieve near-zero training loss (including on the rarest group) but still suffer very low worst-group test accuracy (e.g., 60% on Waterbirds, 41% on CelebA, 66% on MultiNLI). In the vanishing-loss regime, DRO reduces to ERM and offers no worst-case generalization benefit.

Regularization Is Critical for Worst-Group Generalization: Demonstrated that strong explicit (L2 weight decay) or implicit (early stopping) regularization prevents zero training loss, reduces group-specific generalization gaps, and enables Group DRO to substantially improve worst-group test accuracy (by 10–40 percentage points) while retaining high average accuracy. In contrast, ERM under the same regularization sacrifices rare-group performance.

Group Adjustment Further Reduces Worst-Group Error: Showing that adding a term \(C/\sqrt{n_g}\) to each group’s training loss (Group-Adjusted DRO) yields further improvements in worst-group test accuracy (e.g., +5.9% on Waterbirds) by explicitly accounting for differences in expected generalization gaps across groups.

Importance Weighting Is Inadequate for Non-Convex DRO: Empirical and theoretical analysis indicating that reweighting examples by inverse group frequency (a common importance weighting scheme) can improve rare-group performance but does not match Group DRO, especially in non-convex neural networks. Proposition 1 shows that for convex losses there exists a weight distribution that reproduces the DRO solution, but a constructed counterexample demonstrates that no fixed example weights can in general realize the non-convex DRO optimum.

Convergence Guarantee for the Online DRO Algorithm: The proposed interleaved SGD/exponentiated gradient algorithm for Group DRO achieves an expected saddle-point suboptimality \(E[\varepsilon_T]\le 2m\sqrt{10[(B_\theta^2B_\nabla^2)+(B_\ell^2\ln m)]/T}\) under convexity, non-negativity, and Lipschitz conditions (Proposition 2).

techniques.name: Group Distributionally Robust Optimization (Group DRO)
techniques.description: Group Distributionally Robust Optimization (Group DRO) is an end-to-end training procedure designed to learn models that minimize the worst‐case loss across pre‐specified groups, each defined by a spurious attribute–label pairing. In this framework, the training distribution is assumed to factor into m groups P₁,…,Pₘ, and one defines an uncertainty set Q := {∑_{g=1}^m q_g P_g : q∈Δₘ}—the m‐simplex of mixture weights—to capture plausible group shifts. Equivalently, Group DRO solves the minimax problem min_{θ∈Θ} sup_{q∈Δₘ} ∑_{g=1}^m q_g E_{(x,y)∼P_g}[ℓ(θ;(x,y))], which, by linearity of the inner maximization, reduces to minimizing the empirical worst‐group risk \hat R(θ)=max_{g=1…m} E_{(x,y)∼\hat P_g}[ℓ(θ;(x,y))] and selecting θ̂_{DRO}=arg min_{θ∈Θ}\hat R(θ). During training, one assumes access to group labels (so that each training example is of the form (x,y,g)), but not at test time, and interleaves stochastic gradient descent on θ with exponentiated gradient ascent on an adversarial distribution p_adv over groups: at iteration t one samples a group g uniformly, computes ∇_θ ℓ(θ^{(t–1)};(x,y)), updates θ^{(t)}←θ^{(t–1)}–η_θ∇_θℓ(θ^{(t–1)};(x,y)) (using minibatches and momentum in practice), and updates p_adv via exponentiated gradient ascent with step size η_q to reallocate mass toward high‐loss groups. This algorithm admits convergence guarantees in the convex setting, incurs runtime overhead comparable to standard SGD (within 5%), and yields a model whose test‐time performance is evaluated on—and is robust to—the worst‐performing group shift.

techniques.name: Group-Adjusted Distributionally Robust Optimization
techniques.description:
Group‐Adjusted Distributionally Robust Optimization extends the standard group DRO framework by incorporating a data‐driven surrogate for each group’s generalization gap directly into the training objective. Recognizing that each group g has a true generalization gap Δ_g = E_{(x,y)∼P_g}[ℓ(θ;(x,y))] − E_{(x,y)∼ĤP_g}[ℓ(θ;(x,y))], the method approximates this gap with the heuristic ĤΔ_g = C/√n_g, where n_g is the size of group g and C is a model capacity constant treated as a hyperparameter. The adjusted estimator is then defined as 
\hat θ_adj = argmin_{θ∈Θ} max_{g∈𝒢} { E_{(x,y)∼ĤP_g}[ℓ(θ;(x,y))] + C/√n_g }, 
which up‐weights the empirical loss of smaller groups in direct proportion to their estimated overfitting risk. This 1/√n_g scaling, inspired by structural risk minimization and model‐complexity‐based generalization bounds, ensures that groups more prone to overfitting receive greater emphasis during optimization, thereby balancing train‐test performance across groups and improving worst‐group test accuracy.

code.implementation:
import torch
from typing import Any

def compute_group_avg(
    per_sample_losses: torch.Tensor,
    group_idx: torch.Tensor,
    n_groups: int
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Compute the average loss for each group.
    
    Args:
        per_sample_losses (torch.Tensor): Tensor of shape (N,) containing loss values for each sample.
        group_idx (torch.Tensor): Tensor of shape (N,) with integer group indices in [0, n_groups-1].
        n_groups (int): Number of distinct groups.
    
    Returns:
        torch.Tensor: Tensor of shape (n_groups,) where each element is the average loss of that group.
    """
    # One-hot encode group membership
    idx = torch.arange(n_groups, device=group_idx.device).unsqueeze(1)
    mask = (group_idx.unsqueeze(0) == idx).float()  # shape: (n_groups, N)
    counts = mask.sum(dim=1)                         # shape: (n_groups,)
    # Avoid division by zero
    denom = counts + (counts == 0).float()
    # Compute average per-group loss
    group_loss = (mask @ per_sample_losses) / denom
    return group_loss

def group_adjustment_heuristic_loss(
    per_sample_losses: torch.Tensor,
    group_idx: torch.Tensor,
    group_counts: torch.Tensor,
    C: float
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Compute the Group-Adjusted Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) loss.
    
    This method adds a surrogate generalization gap penalty term C/sqrt(n_g)
    to each group's empirical loss and returns the worst-case (maximum) adjusted loss.
    
    Args:
        per_sample_losses (torch.Tensor): Tensor of shape (N,) with loss values for each sample.
        group_idx (torch.Tensor): Tensor of shape (N,) with integer group indices in [0, G-1].
        group_counts (torch.Tensor): Tensor of shape (G,) with the count of samples per group.
        C (float): Capacity hyperparameter controlling the surrogate penalty.
    
    Returns:
        torch.Tensor: A scalar tensor representing the worst-case adjusted group loss.
    """
    n_groups = group_counts.numel()
    # Compute per-group average losses
    group_loss = compute_group_avg(per_sample_losses, group_idx, n_groups)
    # Surrogate generalization penalty: C / sqrt(n_g)
    penalty = C / torch.sqrt(group_counts.float().clamp(min=1.0))
    # Adjust losses and take maximum over groups
    adjusted = group_loss + penalty
    worst_case_loss = adjusted.max()
    return worst_case_loss
code.test:
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Example usage with random data
    N = 1000   # number of samples
    G = 5      # number of groups
    C = 2.0    # capacity hyperparameter

    # Simulate per-sample losses and group assignments
    per_sample_losses = torch.rand(N, dtype=torch.float32)
    group_idx = torch.randint(low=0, high=G, size=(N,), dtype=torch.long)
    # Compute group counts
    group_counts = torch.bincount(group_idx, minlength=G)

    # Compute the DRO loss with group adjustment heuristic
    loss = group_adjustment_heuristic_loss(
        per_sample_losses=per_sample_losses,
        group_idx=group_idx,
        group_counts=group_counts,
        C=C,
    )
    print(f"Worst-case adjusted DRO loss: {loss.item():.4f}")
code.documentation: This module implements the Group-Adjusted Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) loss, which penalizes groups with small sample sizes by adding a term C/√n_g to their empirical loss. The function `compute_group_avg` computes per-group average losses from individual sample losses and group indices. The main function `group_adjustment_heuristic_loss` adds the surrogate generalization penalty to each group’s average loss and returns the worst-case (maximum) adjusted loss across groups. All inputs—including per-sample losses, group indices, group counts, and the capacity parameter C—are externally provided for flexibility. The test block at the end demonstrates how to simulate data and compute this robust loss. This implementation can be plugged directly into training loops where worst-group robustness is required, ensuring that smaller or underrepresented groups receive higher emphasis during optimization.

components.name: Group DRO
components.description:
Group Distributionally Robust Optimization (Group DRO) addresses the problem of learning models that are robust to shifts across \(m\) predefined groups by solving the minimax problem 
\[
\min_{\theta\in\Theta}\;\sup_{q\in\Delta_m}\;\sum_{g=1}^m q_g\,\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim P_g}\bigl[\ell(\theta;(x,y))\bigr],
\]
which, because the supremum over the probability simplex \(\Delta_m\) is attained at a vertex, is equivalent to minimizing the maximum expected loss over the \(m\) groups, 
\[
\mathcal{R}(\theta)=\max_{g\in\{1,\ldots,m\}}\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim P_g}\bigl[\ell(\theta;(x,y))\bigr].
\]
To train this model efficiently on large datasets, one employs an online stochastic optimization algorithm that interleaves gradient‐based updates of the model parameters \(\theta\) with exponentiated gradient ascent on the group weights \(q\). Given step sizes \(\eta_\theta\) and \(\eta_q\), one initializes \(\theta^{(0)}\) and \(q^{(0)}\) uniformly on \(\Delta_m\), and for each iteration \(t=1,\dots,T\) samples a group \(g\sim\mathrm{Uniform}\{1,\dots,m\}\), draws \((x,y)\sim P_g\), forms 
\[
q'_g \gets q^{(t-1)}_g \exp\bigl(\eta_q\,\ell(\theta^{(t-1)};(x,y))\bigr),\quad 
q^{(t)} \gets q'/\sum_{g'}q'_{g'},
\]
and updates the model via 
\[
\theta^{(t)} \gets \theta^{(t-1)} - \eta_\theta\,q^{(t)}_g\,\nabla_\theta \ell\bigl(\theta^{(t-1)};(x,y)\bigr).
\]
In practice, minibatches and a momentum term are used in the \(\theta\)-updates, and the overall runtime per epoch remains within 5 % of standard SGD since the dominant cost is loss and gradient computation. Under standard assumptions that \(\ell\) is nonnegative, convex, \(B_{\mathrm{grad}}\)-Lipschitz, bounded by \(B_{\mathrm{loss}}\), and that \(\|\theta\|_2\le B_\theta\) for all \(\theta\in\Theta\subseteq\mathbb{R}^d\), the average iterate \(\bar\theta^{(1:T)}\) satisfies the \(O(1/\sqrt{T})\) convergence rate 
\[
\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_T]\;\le\;2m\sqrt{\frac{10\bigl(B_\theta^2B_{\mathrm{grad}}^2 + B_{\mathrm{loss}}^2\log m\bigr)}{T}},
\]
where 
\(\varepsilon_T=\max_{q\in\Delta_m}L(\bar\theta^{(1:T)},q)-\min_{\theta\in\Theta}\max_{q\in\Delta_m}L(\theta,q)\) 
and \(L(\theta,q)=\sum_{g=1}^m q_g\,\mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim P_g}[\ell(\theta;(x,y))]\).

components.name: Group Adjustment Heuristic
components.description:
The group-adjustment heuristic augments the empirical groupwise loss in the distributionally robust optimization (DRO) objective by incorporating a surrogate upper bound on the generalization gap, namely \(C/\sqrt{n_g}\), where \(n_g\) denotes the number of training samples in group \(g\) and \(C\) is a capacity hyperparameter. Concretely, one solves 
\[
\hat\theta_{\mathrm{adj}} = \arg\min_{\theta\in\Theta}\;\max_{g\in\mathcal{G}}\;\Bigl\{ \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim\hat P_g}[\ell(\theta;(x,y))] + \frac{C}{\sqrt{n_g}}\Bigr\},
\]
which shifts emphasis towards smaller groups that are more susceptible to overfitting under finite samples. The \(1/\sqrt{n_g}\) scaling is inspired by model-complexity-based generalization bounds and ensures that groups with fewer examples incur larger penalty terms, thereby encouraging the optimizer to reduce their empirical loss more aggressively. \(C\) is tuned, for example by evaluating worst-group validation accuracy across values such as 0, 2, and 4, with \(C=2\) shown to yield balanced group performance in the Waterbirds dataset under strong \(\ell_2\) regularization (\(\lambda=1.0\)). This adjustment applies only in the group-DRO setting—since in ERM the \(1/\sqrt{n_g}\) term is constant across the average loss and does not affect the solution—and directly targets worst-case test loss by prioritizing groups with larger anticipated train–test gaps.

    among all groups.

    Args:
        per_sample_losses (torch.Tensor): Tensor of shape (N,) with loss for each sample.
        group_idx (torch.Tensor): Tensor of shape (N,) with integer group indices in [0, G-1].
        group_counts (torch.Tensor): Tensor of shape (G,) with the number of samples per group.
        C (float): Capacity hyperparameter controlling the surrogate penalty.

    Returns:
        torch.Tensor: A scalar tensor representing the worst-case adjusted group loss.
    """
    n_groups = group_counts.numel()
    # Compute per-group average losses
    group_loss = compute_group_avg(per_sample_losses, group_idx, n_groups)
    # Surrogate generalization penalty: C / sqrt(n_g)
    penalty = C / torch.sqrt(group_counts.float().clamp(min=1.0))
    # Adjusted group losses
    adjusted = group_loss + penalty
    # Worst-case (max) group loss
    worst_case_loss = adjusted.max()
    return worst_case_loss
code.test:
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Example usage with 10 samples and 3 groups
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    N = 10
    G = 3
    # Simulate per-sample losses
    losses = torch.rand(N)
    # Assign each sample to a random group [0, G-1]
    idx = torch.randint(low=0, high=G, size=(N,))
    # Count samples per group
    counts = torch.tensor([int((idx == g).sum().item()) for g in range(G)], dtype=torch.float)
    # Hyperparameter C
    C_val = 2.0

    print("Per-sample losses:", losses)
    print("Group indices:", idx)
    print("Group counts:", counts)

    dro_loss = group_adjustment_heuristic_loss(losses, idx, counts, C_val)
    print("DRO loss with group adjustment heuristic:", dro_loss.item())
code.documentation: This module implements the Group Adjustment Heuristic for DRO, which augments each group's empirical loss by a surrogate penalty C/sqrt(n_g) to account for finite-sample generalization gaps. The function `compute_group_avg` calculates per-group mean losses from per-sample losses and group indices. The main function `group_adjustment_heuristic_loss` computes adjusted group losses by adding the penalty term and returns the worst-case (maximum) among them. All inputs—sample losses, group indices, group counts, and the capacity hyperparameter C—are provided externally, ensuring flexibility. A test block at the end demonstrates usage with random losses and groups, confirming that the code runs standalone. This implementation can be directly used in training loops where group-wise worst-case robustness is desired.

techniques.name: Online Stochastic Optimization Algorithm for Group DRO
techniques.description: The online stochastic optimization algorithm for group DRO proceeds by initializing the model parameters θ^(0) ∈ Θ and the adversarial group weights q^(0) ∈ Δ_m, and employs step sizes η_q and η_θ together with the known group distributions P_g for g = 1,…,m. At each iteration t = 1,…,T the algorithm samples a group g uniformly at random from {1,…,m} and then draws an example (x,y)∼P_g. It first sets q′ ← q^(t−1) and performs an exponentiated gradient ascent update on the chosen group weight via q′_g ← q′_g exp(η_q ℓ(θ^(t−1);(x,y))), thereafter renormalizing q^(t) ← q′ / ∑_{g′}q′_{g′}. It then updates the model parameters by stochastic gradient descent with a weighted gradient, θ^(t) ← θ^(t−1) − η_θ q_g^(t) ∇_θ ℓ(θ^(t−1);(x,y)). In practice, minibatches and a momentum term are used for the θ‐updates, and the overall runtime per epoch remains within 5 % of standard SGD since the dominant cost is loss and gradient computation. Under the assumptions that ℓ(·;(x,y)) is non‐negative, convex, B_grad‐Lipschitz continuous and bounded by B_loss, and that ∥θ∥_2 ≤ B_θ for all θ ∈ Θ ⊂ ℝ^d, the average iterate of this algorithm achieves an expected saddle‐point error bounded by E[ε_T] ≤ 2m√[10 (B_θ^2 B_grad^2 + B_loss^2 log m)/T], yielding the O(1/√T) convergence rate stated in Proposition 2.
